context: Current closing price of Intuit (INTU): $404.85 USD as of 2026-04-21.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a relatively stable near-term trajectory with modest upward drift over longer horizons. For the 1-day estimate of 407, I was anchoring closely to recent observed levels, assuming mean-reverting behavior and continuity from the prevailing trend. The 1-week estimate of 410 incorporates a slight positive drift, reflecting whatever underlying momentum or fundamental bias I assessed at the time of the forecast. The 1-month estimate of 415 extends that gradual trend further, though with diminishing confidence that the same dynamics persist over a longer window. In each case, the point estimates are essentially a projection of recent trajectory plus a small expected drift, without assuming any major structural breaks.

The confidence interval widths widen substantially as the horizon extends, which reflects the compounding of uncertainty over time. At 1 day, the 34-unit range from 390 to 424 is relatively tight, reflecting that short-term price or value changes tend to be bounded by intraday or overnight volatility. At 1 week, the 64-unit range captures the possibility of a meaningful shift in sentiment, incoming data releases, or technical breakouts. By 1 month, the 130-unit range acknowledges that many more events can intervene — policy decisions, macroeconomic data, geopolitical developments, or shifts in market regime — any of which could push outcomes meaningfully away from the trend.

Specific risks that could push outcomes outside my stated intervals include sharp, unexpected macro shocks such as a surprise central bank decision, a significant inflation print, or an abrupt deterioration in credit or liquidity conditions. On the upside, an unexpectedly strong economic data release or a dovish policy pivot could drive outcomes above the upper bounds, particularly at the 1-week and 1-month horizons. On the downside, a sudden risk-off event, geopolitical escalation, or financial contagion could push outcomes well below the lower bounds. These tail risks are real but were judged unlikely enough at the time to fall outside my 90% confidence intervals, though they are certainly not impossible.

It is also worth noting that the intervals themselves carry model uncertainty — meaning my distributional assumptions may not fully capture fat-tailed behavior or structural breaks. In practice, observed outcomes in financial or economic series tend to have heavier tails than normal distributions imply, so the true probability of extreme outcomes may be somewhat higher than the 10% I implicitly assigned to the regions outside my stated bounds.
